remedies.remedy_desc: Peppermint tea helps with cough because it contains menthol, a natural decongestant that helps to thin mucus and soothe sore throats. The warm temperature of the tea can also help to alleviate congestion by increasing the movement of mucus and reducing inflammation in the throat. Additionally, peppermint tea has antispasmodic properties, which can help to relax the muscles of the trachea and reduce coughing fits. Furthermore, peppermint tea is caffeine-free and can be consumed in large quantities without the risk of causing jitteriness or interfering with sleep, making it a safe and natural remedy for coughs. Finally, peppermint tea has a calming effect on the mind and body, which can help to reduce stress and promote relaxation, further aiding in the relief of cough symptoms.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of the peppermint plant are widely used in medicinal teas for cough due to their high menthol content, which helps to soothe irritated throats and thin mucus.
Flowers: The flowers of the peppermint plant are used in teas for their calming properties, which can help to alleviate symptoms of cough and colds.
Stems: The stems of the peppermint plant contain rosmarinic acid, which has anti-inflammatory properties that can help to reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract and relieve cough.
remedy_constituents: Menthol: Acts as a mild sedative and expectorant, helping to soothe coughs and loosen phlegm.
Rosmarinic acid: Has anti-inflammatory properties that may help reduce irritation in the throat and suppress coughing.
Limonene: Has been shown to have antimicrobial properties, which can help fight off infections causing coughs.
Menthone: Helps to relax muscles, including the trachea, making it easier to breathe and reducing coughing.
Cineol: Has expectorant properties that help to clear mucus and reduce coughing.
Vitamin A: Supports the immune system, which can help the body fight off infections causing coughs.
remedy_recipe: In a teapot, add boiling water (1 cup) to dried peppermint leaves (1 tablespoon).
Optional: add honey (1 teaspoon) for added sweetness and cough-suppressing properties.
Sip the tea while still warm, repeating as needed every few hours.

remedies.remedy_desc: Licorice tea helps with cough because it contains a compound called glycyrrhizin, which helps soothe inflammation in the throat and reduce irritation. This substance also helps loosen and expel phlegm, making it easier to expel mucus and reduce the duration of a cough. Moreover, licorice tea has expectorant properties, which means it promotes the production of thin and watery mucus that can be easily eliminated from the body. Additionally, it has a soothing effect on the throat and helps relieve pain and discomfort caused by persistent coughing. However, it is important to consume licorice tea in moderation, as excessive consumption can cause side effects such as high blood pressure and potassium depletion.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the licorice plant contain glycyrrhizin, which helps to reduce inflammation and soothe coughs.
Rhizomes: The rhizomes of the licorice plant have expectorant properties, aiding in the expulsion of mucus from the respiratory tract and alleviating cough.
Leaves: The leaves of the licorice plant contain flavonoids that have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, helpful in treating coughs and respiratory ailments.
Flowers: The flowers of the licorice plant contain essential oils and flavonoids that have soothing and calming effects on the throat, reducing cough.
remedy_constituents: Glycyrrhizin: This compound is the primary active ingredient in licorice, and has been shown to have anti-tussive (cough-suppressing) properties.
Liquiritigenin: This flavonoid found in licorice has been found to have expectorant effects, helping to clear mucus from the airways and alleviate coughs.
Licochalcone A: This compound has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce inflammation in the airways and alleviate coughing.
Isoliquiritigenin: This flavonoid has been found to have antiviral properties, and may help to reduce the severity and duration of coughs caused by viral infections.
Glabridin: This compound has been shown to have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, which may help to reduce inflammation and irritation in the airways and alleviate coughing.
remedy_recipe: In a pot, boil 1 cup of water for 3-5 minutes.
Add 1 teaspoon of dried licorice root and reduce heat to a simmer.
Drink the tea, up to three times a day, for cough relief.

remedies.remedy_desc: Slippery elm tea helps with cough because it contains mucilage, a substance that becomes a slick gel when mixed with water. This gel can coat and soothe a sore throat, reducing the need to cough. Furthermore, the gel can also moisturize the throat and airways, making it easier to cough up mucus and phlegm. Additionally, slippery elm tea has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the throat and airways, further reducing the incidence of coughing fits. Lastly, the tea may also stimulate the production of saliva and stomach juices, which can help break down mucus and phlegm, making it easier to expel. Overall, slippery elm tea offers a multifaceted approach to managing coughs, making it a popular natural remedy for respiratory ailments.
remedy_parts: Bark: The bark of the slippery elm plant is commonly used for medicinal teas for cough due to its high mucilage content, which can soothe irritated throats and help reduce coughing.

Inner Bark: The inner bark is often used in teas, as it contains a variety of beneficial compounds, including antioxidants and anti-inflammatory agents, which can help alleviate cough and related symptoms.
Leaves: Slippery elm leaves are used in teas for their demulcent properties, helping to soothe and protect mucous membranes in the respiratory tract, thereby alleviating cough and irritation.

Flowers: While less commonly used, slippery elm flowers can also be included in teas, as they contain flavonoids and other beneficial compounds that can help reduce inflammation and relieve cough symptoms.
remedy_constituents: Mucilage: The mucilage in slippery elm bark becomes a slick, gel-like substance when mixed with water. This coats and soothes irritated membranes throughout the respiratory tract, providing relief from irritating coughs.

Tannins: Slippery elm bark contains tannins, which have astringent properties that help to reduce inflammation and promote healing. These astringent qualities can help alleviate coughs by reducing irritation and swelling in the throat and upper respiratory tract.

Flavonoids: Flavonoids in slippery elm bark have antioxidant properties and can help protect the respiratory system from damage caused by free radicals. They may help reduce inflammation associated with coughs, making it easier to breathe.

Starch: Starch in slippery elm bark adds to the mucilage's soothing effect. When combined with water, it forms a thick, soothing gel that coats and soothes irritated mucous membranes, thus alleviating throat irritation and reducing coughs.

Essential oil: Slippery elm essential oil contains several components, such as palmitic, stearic, and oleic acids. These components have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, providing relief for inflamed respiratory tissue and reducing coughing.
Sterols: Sterols in slippery elm bark are known to have anti-inflammatory effects. They can help reduce the inflammation in the airways responsible for causing coughs and difficult breathing.

Saponins: Saponins have been shown to have expectorant and antitussive properties, which can help alleviate coughs by reducing the accumulation of mucus in the respiratory tract, making it easier to expel and breathe.
remedy_recipe: In a pot, combine 1 cup of water and 1 teaspoon of dried slippery elm bark.
Bring the mixture to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer for 10 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove bark particles, then let it cool to a drinkable temperature.
Stir in honey to taste, if desired, for added sweetness and cough-suppressing properties.
Sip the tea slowly, allowing it to coat your throat and soothe your cough.

remedies.remedy_desc: Marshmallow tea helps with cough because it contains a substance called mucilage, a slimy mixture that coats the throat and soothes irritation. This coating provides a protective barrier against cough-inducing irritants and reduces the frequency and severity of coughing fits. Furthermore, marshmallow tea's anti-inflammatory properties help alleviate inflammation in the respiratory tract, providing additional relief from continuous coughing. Marshmallow tea is also known to promote mucus production, which helps clear out phlegm and other irritants that may be causing the cough. Lastly, the soothing and calming effects of marshmallow tea can help improve sleep quality, an essential factor in the recovery process, especially for individuals suffering from cold or flu-related coughs.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of the marshmallow plant contain mucilage, which can help soothe a sore throat and reduce coughing.
Flowers: Marshmallow flowers also contain mucilage, making them a valuable addition to medicinal tea for cough due to their soothing properties.
Roots: The roots of the marshmallow plant are rich in mucilage, which can help relieve irritation and inflammation in the respiratory tract, providing relief for coughs.
Bark: The bark of the marshmallow plant contains high levels of mucilage, making it an effective ingredient in medicinal tea for cough, as it can help reduce irritation in the throat and lungs.
remedy_constituents: Mucilage: The mucilage in marshmallow coats the throat and soothes irritation, providing relief from coughing.
Polysaccharides: The polysaccharides, including pectin and hemicellulose, have demulcent properties that help reduce irritation and inflammation in the respiratory tract, alleviating coughs.
Flavonoids: Marshmallow contains flavonoids like quercetin, which have anti-inflammatory properties, helping to soothe and reduce coughs caused by inflammation.
Aspirin-like compounds: Marshmallow contains small amounts of salicylates, which can help relieve pain, reduce inflammation, and alleviate coughs.
Tannins: The tannins in marshmallow have astringent properties, helping to tighten and protect mucous membranes, providing relief from coughing.
remedy_recipe: In a pot, pour 2 cups of water and bring to a boil.
Stir in 2-3 large marshmallows, reduce heat, and simmer for 5 minutes.
Remove from heat, add 1 teaspoon of lemon juice and 1/4 teaspoon of honey.
Steep for 10 minutes, then strain the tea to remove marshmallow solids.
Serve hot, and enjoy the soothing effect on your cough.

remedies.remedy_desc: Thyme tea helps with cough because it contains antispasmodic and expectorant properties, which means it can help relax the muscles of the trachea and ease coughing fits, as well as thin and expel mucus from the respiratory tract. The primary active compound in thyme, called thymol, has been found to have antitussive and antibacterial effects, further supporting its use in treating coughs and respiratory infections. Additionally, thyme tea has been traditionally used as a natural remedy for respiratory ailments, and some studies suggest that it may be just as effective as over-the-counter cough suppressants. Furthermore, thyme tea is a caffeine-free and generally safe alternative to traditional cold and cough medicines, making it a popular choice for those looking for a more natural approach to managing their symptoms. However, it is always best to consult with a healthcare provider before starting any new treatment regimen.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Thyme leaves are commonly used in medicinal tea for cough due to their natural antitussive and expectorant properties.
Flowers: Thyme flowers contain flavonoids that help soothe coughs and reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract.
Stems: Although less potent than leaves and flowers, thyme stems may still contribute some beneficial compounds when making medicinal tea for cough.
remedy_constituents: Thymol: Thymol has antitussive properties that help to reduce coughing by acting on the cough center in the brain.
Carvacrol: Carvacrol has expectorant properties that help to loosen and expel mucus from the respiratory tract, relieving cough.
Flavonoids: Flavonoids in thyme have anti-inflammatory properties that help to reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, providing relief from cough and other symptoms of respiratory infections.
Volatile oils: The volatile oils in thyme have antimicrobial properties that help to kill the bacteria and viruses that can cause respiratory infections, reducing the severity and duration of cough.
Tannins: Tannins in thyme have astringent properties that help to soothe irritated tissue in the respiratory tract, providing relief from cough and other symptoms of respiratory infections.
remedy_recipe: In a small pot, bring 1 cup of water to a boil, which takes approximately 3-5 minutes.
Add 1 teaspoon of dried thyme leaves or 1 tablespoon of fresh thyme to the boiling water.
Reduce heat and let it simmer for 5-7 minutes to allow the thyme to release its medicinal properties.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the used thyme leaves.
Sip the warm thyme tea, ideally three times a day, for soothing relief of a cough.

remedies.remedy_desc: Mullein tea helps with cough because it contains mucilage, a substance that becomes a slick gel when mixed with water. This gel can soothe and protect irritated mucous membranes in the throat and respiratory tract, reducing the need to cough. Additionally, mullein has expectorant properties that help loosen and expel thick mucus, making it easier to cough up and expel. The tea is also anti-inflammatory, which can help reduce inflammation in the airways and further alleviate coughing. Furthermore, mullein has been found to have calming effects on the nervous system, which can help reduce the severity and frequency of coughing fits. Finally, mullein tea is a natural and caffeine-free alternative to over-the-counter cough medicines, making it a safe and gentle option for people of all ages.
remedy_parts: Flowers: Rich in mucilage, flowers help soothe irritated throats and reduce coughing.
Leaves: Containing saponins, leaves can help loosen phlegm and provide relief from a persistent cough.
Roots: With Expectorant properties, roots aid in eliminating mucus from the respiratory tract, alleviating a cough.
remedy_constituents: Verbascoside: This phenylethanoid glycoside has been shown to have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce irritation in the respiratory tract and alleviate coughing.

Iridoids: Mullein contains iridoid glycosides such as aucubin and catalpol, which have been found to have expectorant and soothing effects on the mucous membranes, helping to relieve coughs and clear phlegm.
Sitosterol: A type of plant sterol, sitosterol has been suggested to have anti-inflammatory and immune-modulating effects, potentially aiding in the treatment of respiratory infections and coughs.

Flavonoids: Mullein contains flavonoids, such as rutin and quercetin, that have been shown to have anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antiviral properties, which can support respiratory health and alleviate coughs.

Mucilage: Mullein leaves contain mucilaginous compounds that form a soothing, demulcent film when in contact with moisture. This film helps to protect and lubricate mucous membranes, reducing irritation and coughing.
Saponins: Saponins present in mullein may have expectorant and secretolytic activities, aiding in the expulsion of mucus and phlegm from the respiratory tract and helping to relieve coughs.
remedy_recipe: Heat one cup of water to boiling point (10 minutes) for each serving of tea.
Add one teaspoon of dried mullein leaves (2-3 minutes) to the boiling water.
Reduce heat and let the tea simmer (10-15 minutes) for optimal extraction of medicinal compounds.
Strain the tea to remove mullein leaves (1 minute) and discard them.
Drink the tea, up to three times a day (5 minutes), for soothing relief of cough and irritated throat.

remedies.remedy_desc: Coltsfoot tea helps with cough because it contains mucilage, a substance that coats the throat and alleviates irritation, thereby reducing persistent coughing. Additionally, it has anti-inflammatory properties that help soothe inflamed airways, making it easier to expel mucus and phlegm. The tea also contains expectorant and demulcent qualities, which help loosen mucus and provide a soothing effect on the throat, further alleviating coughing fits. Furthermore, coltsfoot tea's natural bronchodilator properties can help open up the airways, making it easier to breathe and reducing the frequency and intensity of coughing. Lastly, coltsfoot tea has been used traditionally for its calming effects on the nervous system, which can help reduce stress and anxiety that can exacerbate coughing, providing further relief to those suffering from a cough.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of coltsfoot are commonly used in medicinal teas for cough because they contain mucilage, which can help soothe irritated throats.
Flowers: The flowers of coltsfoot are used in teas for coughs due to their anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties, which can help clear congestion.
Buds: Coltsfoot buds are rich in therapeutic compounds and are included in tea blends for coughs as they can help alleviate irritation and expel mucus.
Rhyzomes: The rhyzomes of coltsfoot, a horizontal underground stem, are used in teas for cough relief because they contain high levels of mucilage and antitussive compounds.
remedy_constituents: Mucilage: Coltsfoot contains mucilage, a substance that coats the throat and soothes irritation, helping to reduce coughing.
Zinc: This mineral present in coltsfoot can help reduce inflammation and strengthen the immune response, which may help alleviate coughing caused by respiratory infections.
Tannins: The tannins in coltsfoot have astringent properties, helping to tighten and protect mucous membranes, which can provide relief from a persistent cough.
Flavonoids: Flavonoids, such as rutin and quercetin, found in coltsfoot, have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, helping to reduce inflammation and irritation in the respiratory tract.
Pectin: Pectin, another constituent of coltsfoot, has demulcent properties, forming a soothing film over mucous membranes, helping to alleviate cough and irritation.
remedy_recipe: Harvest: Pick 2-3 handfuls of Coltsfoot leaves, ensuring they are dry and free of pollution, typically in early spring.
Rinse: Rinelyse the leaves gently under cold running water to eliminate dirt and other impurities, then let them air dry.
Chop: Finely chop the dried coltsfoot leaves into small pieces using a sharp knife or herb scissors to increase the surface area for steeping.
Steep: Submerge the chopped coltsfoot leaves in 1 quart (4 cups) of boiling water and allow the tea to steep for 10-15 minutes, covered.
Strain: Strain the solid leaves from the tea using a fine-mesh strainer or cheesecloth, squeezing out any remaining liquid, and discard the spent plant material.

remedies.remedy_desc: Elecampane tea helps with cough because it contains a compound called alantolactone, which has expectorant and anti-inflammatory properties that help to loosen phlegm and soothe irritated throat tissues. Furthermore, elecampane has been found to have antibacterial properties, which can help eliminate the bacteria causing the cough. Additionally, it helps to stimulate the production of immune-boosting white blood cells, providing extra protection against respiratory infections that can lead to coughing fits. Additionally, elecampane tea can help to relax the muscles in the airways, reducing the frequency and severity of coughing spasms. Finally, elecampane has been used traditionally as a cough remedy for centuries, and its effectiveness has been supported by some modern scientific studies, making it a natural and trusted alternative for cough relief.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of elecampane contain high amounts of anti-tussive compounds, making them effective at soothing coughs.
Rhizomes: The rhizomes of elecampane are rich in mucilage, which helps to coat and soothe irritated throat membranes.
Flowers: The flowers of elecampane contain essential oils with expectorant properties, aiding in the expulsion of mucus from the respiratory tract.
Leaves: The leaves of elecampane have demulcent properties, providing a soothing effect on inflamed mucous membranes.
Bark: The bark of elecampane contains compounds with antispasmodic properties, helping to relieve coughing spasms.
remedy_constituents: Isoverein: This compound is a type of antitussive, meaning it helps to reduce coughing by acting on the cough reflex.
Inulin: Inulin has expectorant properties, which help to thin and loosen mucus in the airways, making it easier to cough up and get rid of.
Helenalin: This compound has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help to reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract and alleviate coughing.
Eudesmanolides: These compounds are known to have bronchodilator effects, which means they can help to widen the airways and improve breathing, reducing the need to cough.
Flavonoids: Flavonoids have antioxidant properties, which can help to protect the respiratory tract from damage and reduce inflammation, thereby alleviating coughing.
remedy_recipe: Chop: Cut 1-2 tablespoons of dried elecampane root into small pieces.
Measure: Boil 1 cup of water in a pot and add the chopped elecampane root.
Simmer: Let it simmer on low heat for 10-15 minutes.
Strain: Strain the tea into a cup using a fine mesh strainer or cheesecloth.
Serve: Drink the tea while warm, up to three times a day for cough relief.

remedies.remedy_desc: Pleurisy tea helps with cough because it contains expectorant properties that aid in thinning and removing mucus from the lungs, making it easier to cough up and expel. The tea's anti-inflammatory properties also help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, alleviating irritation and discomfort that can exacerbate coughing fits. Additionally, pleurisy tea is rich in antioxidants that support the immune system and help the body fight off infections that may be causing the cough. The soothing warmth of the tea can also help to calm irritated throat tissues, providing relief and reducing the frequency and intensity of coughing fits. Furthermore, the natural calming effects of pleurisy tea can help to promote relaxation and improve sleep quality, allowing the body to rest and heal more effectively, which can also contribute to a reduction in coughing.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of the pleurisy plant are used to make medicinal tea for cough due to their anti-tussive and expectorant properties.
Flowers: The flowers of the pleurisy plant are utilized in tea preparation for cough relief, as they contain flavonoids with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects.
Bark: The bark of the pleurisy plant is included in medicinal tea for cough treatment, as it has been found to contain compounds with soothing effects on the respiratory tract.
remedy_constituents: Marrubiin: Acts as an expectorant, helping to loosen and expel mucus from the airways, thus alleviating cough.
Oleanolic Acid: Demonstrates anti-tussive properties, directly helping to suppress cough.
remedy_recipe: In a saucepan, combine 1 teaspoon of dried pleurisy root, 1 teaspoon of dried licorice root, and 2 cups of water.
Bring the mixture to a boil, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 15-20 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the herbs, then add honey and lemon to taste.
Drink 2-3 cups of this tea daily to soothe a cough related to pleurisy or other respiratory conditions.

remedies.remedy_desc: Grindelia tea helps with cough because it contains antispasmodic and anti-inflammatory properties that soothe the respiratory tract, reducing the frequency and intensity of coughing fits. The tea's active compounds, such as grindelic acid and resin, relax the muscles of the trachea and bronchi, alleviating irritation and inflammation in the throat. Furthermore, Grindelia tea acts as an expectorant, assisting in the elimination of mucus and phlegm from the lungs, thereby easing congestion and making it easier to breathe. Its calming effects on the nervous system also help to reduce anxiety and tension associated with persistent coughing, making it a well-rounded natural remedy for addressing both the physical and psychological aspects of cough. However, it is essential to consult with a healthcare professional before using Grindelia tea to ensure it is safe and suitable for your specific health condition.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of the grindelia plant contain therapeutic compounds, known for their anti-tussive and expectorant properties, helping to alleviate coughs and expel mucus from the respiratory tract.
Flowers: Grindelia flowers are rich in soothing mucilage, making them an ideal addition to medicinal teas aimed at calming coughs and reducing irritation in the throat.

Roots: The roots of the grindelia plant have been used traditionally due to their potent anti-inflammatory and cough-suppressing effects, providing relief from persistent coughs and inflammation in the respiratory system.
remedy_constituents: Pinosylvin: A type of stilbenoid that has anti-inflammatory and antitussive (cough-suppressing) properties.
Resin: Contains terpenes that have expectorant and soothing effects on the respiratory tract, helping to alleviate cough.
Volatile oils: Including cineole, which has antitussive, expectorant, and anti-inflammatory properties, providing relief for cough and inflammation in the respiratory tract.
Tannins: Astringent compounds that help to soothe irritated mucous membranes and reduce coughing.
Saponins: These compounds can help to loosen and expel mucus, making it easier to expel phlegm and alleviating cough.
remedy_recipe: Heat 2 cups of water in a pot until it reaches a boiling point.
Add 1-2 teaspoons of dried Grindelia (Grindelia robusta) leaves and flowers to the boiling water.
Drink the tea while warm, up to three times a day for cough relief.
